title: Lung Abscess
oneLiner: Contained necrotic lung cavity from infection (usually anaerobic) – presents with fever, foul sputum, and needs prolonged antibiotics and exclusion of malignancy.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Massive haemoptysis:** single cough with >200ml blood; immediate sign: active bright red blood from mouth, haemodynamic instability → call senior, cross-match, consider bronchial artery embolisation.
- **Tension pneumothorax from bronchopleural fistula:** sudden-onset severe dyspnoea, tracheal deviation away, hyper-resonance, distended neck veins → immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) – this is the rare but lethal mimic of a simple abscess.
- **Septic shock from rupture into pleural space:** new pleuritic pain, swinging fever, hypotension, rising lactate – differentiate from simple abscess by signs of empyema (dullness, reduced breath sounds, pleural rub) and need for urgent chest drain.
sections.Presentation:
- Foul-smelling sputum (often described as “rotten egg” or “fetid”) – key historical clue for anaerobic infection
- Productive cough (green/brown sputum, may be blood-streaked)
- High fever with rigors, night sweats
- Pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea
- Weight loss, malaise (overlap with TB/malignancy)
- Signs of poor dental hygiene / aspiration risk (reduced consciousness, alcohol misuse, dysphagia)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Empyema (complicated parapneumonic effusion):** loculated pleural fluid, not true lung cavity – differentiate by ultrasound/CT: empyema shows “split pleura” sign, no air-fluid level unless bronchopleural fistula is present.
- **Lung cancer (cavitating SCC):** usually no fever, weight loss dominant, sputum cytology positive for malignant cells, no foul odour.
- **Tuberculosis (TB):** apical cavity, chronic cough, night sweats, no foul sputum, positive IGRA/sputum AFB, immunocompromised risk.
- **Necrotising pneumonia (rapidly progressive):** multiple small cavities, rapid clinical decline (high fever, hypoxia), often staph/klebsiella – CXR shows diffuse consolidation with early cavitation.
- **Fungal infection (e.g. aspergilloma):** pre-existing cavity (TB, sarcoid), haemoptysis, no foul sputum, IgE/CT halo sign.

**Splitter:** Foul-smelling sputum + poor dentition/aspiration risk → lung abscess (anaerobic). No foul odour + apical cavity + immunocompromised → TB or fungal.
sections.Investigations:
**Bedside (within 1 hour):**
- Check NEWS2, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate
- Blood cultures (two sets before antibiotics if not septic shock)
- Sputum for culture and sensitivity – but don’t delay antibiotics if septic

**Bloods:**
- FBC (neutrophilia), CRP, procalcitonin (elevated suggests bacterial)
- U&Es, LFTs (albumin low chronic infection), clotting (if drain planned)
- Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic)
- Consider: IGRA/T-SPOT (TB risk), HIV serology (in persistent/recurrent abscess)

**Imaging:**
- **First-line:** CXR PA and lateral – shows cavity with air-fluid level >2cm, often in posterior segments of upper lobes or superior segments of lower lobes (aspiration-prone areas). Look for associated consolidation or effusion.
- **Second-line:** CT chest with IV contrast (within 24-48h) – confirms cavity, differentiates from empyema, rules out endobronchial lesion (tumour, foreign body). Also assesses for bronchopleural fistula.
- **If TB suspected:** CT thorax with high-resolution windows + sputum AFB/NAAT.

**Special tests:**
- Bronchoscopy (if no improvement after 7-10 days of antibiotics, or if suspicion of malignancy/foreign body) – obtain BAL for culture, cytology, TB.

**Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated scoring system. Diagnosis relies on CXR/CT demonstration of a lung cavity with air-fluid level plus consistent clinical picture. Provideer criteria: >2cm cavity on CT, plus appropriate clinical setting (aspiration, fever, foul sputum).

**Never-miss test:** **CT chest with contrast** – the ONE investigation whose omission risks missing underlying malignancy or empyema, leading to failed treatment and delayed diagnosis.
sections.Management:
**Immediate (first hour / ED):**
- If septic: IV fluids (crystalloid, 500ml bolus, reassess), start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (see Drugs section) after blood cultures – but do not delay >1 hour in sepsis.
- Ensure sputum sent for culture; if unable to produce, consider induced sputum.
- If massive haemoptysis: call senior, cross-match, consider bronchial artery embolisation – do not bronchoscope unstable patient.
- If tension pneumothorax: immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS, midclavicular line) followed by chest drain.

**Ward (first 24-72h):**
- Continue IV antibiotics for at least 72h or until afebrile and clinically improving.
- Chest physiotherapy with postural drainage (head-down, affected side up) – but only if no bronchopleural fistula or massive haemoptysis.
- Monitor sputum volume, colour, fever curve, and NEWS2 daily.
- If no improvement (fever, rising CRP) by day 7: re-image with CT, consider bronchoscopy, check for drug resistance or empyema.
- Consider CT-guided drainage if abscess >4cm and not responding to medical therapy (rare – most resolve with antibiotics alone).

**Long-term / outpatient:**
- Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile >48h and clinically stable (see Drugs section).
- Total treatment duration: **4-6 weeks** (at least 2 weeks IV, then oral).
- Arrange follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks to confirm cavity resolution.
- Refer to respiratory physician if underlying malignancy or structural lung disease identified.

**Default Orders (if admitted):**
- **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 1ml/kg/h (if not in fluid overload) – adjust based on clinical state.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk (massive haemoptysis – withhold, discuss with senior).
- **Diet:** Full diet, encourage high-calorie/high-protein (weight loss common).
- **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 initially; increase to hourly if NEWS2 ≥5.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
**First-line agents (per BNF/UK practice):**
- **IV co-amoxiclav** (amoxicillin/clavulanate) 1.2g IV tds (if normal renal function) – covers anaerobes and gram-positive/negative.
- **Alternative if penicillin allergy:** IV clindamycin 600mg IV qds (anti-anaerobic) + IV cefuroxime 750mg tds (gram-negative cover) – but check local policy for dosing.
- **If severe sepsis or aspiration pneumonia with poor dentition:** IV piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g tds – covers Pseudomonas and anaerobes.
- **Oral switch:** co-amoxiclav 625mg po tds or doxycycline 100mg po bd (if penicillin allergic) – continue for total of 4-6 weeks.

**Common alternatives:**
- Metronidazole 400mg po tds (add if anaerobes suspected and not on co-amoxiclav) – but clindamycin is more complete.
- Levofloxacin 500mg po od (reserve for severe allergies or resistance).

**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment (check LFTs), risk of cholestatic jaundice (especially elderly, courses >14 days).
- Clindamycin: risk of C. difficile colitis – monitor for diarrhoea.
- Piperacillin/tazobactam: high sodium load (4.5g contains 8mmol Na) – caution in heart failure.

**Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
- **Co-amoxiclav:** reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 1.2g IV stat then 600mg IV bd, or 375mg po bd). Check BNF.
- **Piperacillin/tazobactam:** adjust if eGFR <20 – [check local policy].
- **Clindamycin:** no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe liver disease (reduce dose).

**Most dangerous dose error:** **Not covering anaerobes** – giving only a cephalosporin or macrolide without anaerobic cover fails to treat the majority of lung abscesses, leading to clinical deterioration and empyema. Always add metronidazole or use co-amoxiclav.
sections.Complications:
**Acute:**
- **Massive haemoptysis** (from erosion into bronchial artery) – can be fatal.
- **Bronchopleural fistula** → tension pneumothorax or empyema – need chest drain +/- surgery.
- **Empyema** (if abscess ruptures into pleural space) – requires chest drainage.
- **Septic emboli to brain** (especially if right-to-left shunt, e.g. from PFO) – look for neurological symptoms.

**Chronic:**
- Chronic cavity with persistent cough, weight loss, recurrent infections.
- Fibrothorax (if empyema complicated).
- Bronchiectasis (from chronic inflammation).
sections.Prognosis: - With appropriate intravenous antibiotics (4-6 weeks), >90% of uncomplicated lung abscesses resolve without drainage. Mortality ~5-10% overall, higher in elderly, immunocompromised, or if underlying malignancy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always examine the mouth** – poor dentition and gingivitis are the commonest sources of anaerobic bacteria in lung abscess. Document and consider dental consult.
- **If no improvement by day 7, escalate imaging** – CT is mandatory to exclude an endobronchial lesion (tumour/foreign body) or empyema that requires drainage.
- **Send sputum for culture before antibiotics** – but never delay antibiotics if septic; culture is often negative anyway (anaerobic difficult to culture). If negative, clinical response is key.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Using antibiotics without anaerobic cover** – lung abscess is almost always anaerobic; a cephalosporin or macrolide alone is insufficient. Always add metronidazole or use co-amoxiclav.
- **Stopping antibiotics early** – total course must be 4-6 weeks (IV then oral). Short courses (e.g. 7-10 days) risk relapse and chronic cavity.
- **Assuming all cavities are infection – not checking for underlying malignancy** – every patient with a lung abscess requires CT to exclude cancer, especially in smokers over 40. Do not attribute all symptoms to infection without imaging.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening shortness of breath or haemoptysis (any amount)
  - Fever returns after being afebrile
  - New pleuritic chest pain or increasing cough with foul sputum
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4-6 weeks; repeat CXR at 6-8 weeks to confirm cavity closure. If cavity persists >12 weeks, consider bronchoscopy.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 with hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on FiO2 0.5) or rising lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Signs of massive haemoptysis (>200ml) or hypotension not responding to fluids
  - GCS drop <13 or new confusion
  - Suspected bronchopleural fistula (sudden dyspnoea, subcutaneous emphysema, new pneumothorax)
- **Specific thresholds:** Consider ICU referral if PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 0.5, or RR >30 despite best medical therapy. For haemoptysis, activate massive haemorrhage protocol (cross-match, call interventional radiology/surgery).

rawMarkdown:
## Lung Abscess

**One-liner:** Contained necrotic lung cavity from infection (usually anaerobic) – presents with fever, foul sputum, and needs prolonged antibiotics and exclusion of malignancy.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Massive haemoptysis:** single cough with >200ml blood; immediate sign: active bright red blood from mouth, haemodynamic instability → call senior, cross-match, consider bronchial artery embolisation.
- **Tension pneumothorax from bronchopleural fistula:** sudden-onset severe dyspnoea, tracheal deviation away, hyper-resonance, distended neck veins → immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS mid-clavicular line) – this is the rare but lethal mimic of a simple abscess.
- **Septic shock from rupture into pleural space:** new pleuritic pain, swinging fever, hypotension, rising lactate – differentiate from simple abscess by signs of empyema (dullness, reduced breath sounds, pleural rub) and need for urgent chest drain.

### Presentation
- Foul-smelling sputum (often described as “rotten egg” or “fetid”) – key historical clue for anaerobic infection
- Productive cough (green/brown sputum, may be blood-streaked)
- High fever with rigors, night sweats
- Pleuritic chest pain, dyspnoea
- Weight loss, malaise (overlap with TB/malignancy)
- Signs of poor dental hygiene / aspiration risk (reduced consciousness, alcohol misuse, dysphagia)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Empyema (complicated parapneumonic effusion):** loculated pleural fluid, not true lung cavity – differentiate by ultrasound/CT: empyema shows “split pleura” sign, no air-fluid level unless bronchopleural fistula is present.
- **Lung cancer (cavitating SCC):** usually no fever, weight loss dominant, sputum cytology positive for malignant cells, no foul odour.
- **Tuberculosis (TB):** apical cavity, chronic cough, night sweats, no foul sputum, positive IGRA/sputum AFB, immunocompromised risk.
- **Necrotising pneumonia (rapidly progressive):** multiple small cavities, rapid clinical decline (high fever, hypoxia), often staph/klebsiella – CXR shows diffuse consolidation with early cavitation.
- **Fungal infection (e.g. aspergilloma):** pre-existing cavity (TB, sarcoid), haemoptysis, no foul sputum, IgE/CT halo sign.

**Splitter:** Foul-smelling sputum + poor dentition/aspiration risk → lung abscess (anaerobic). No foul odour + apical cavity + immunocompromised → TB or fungal.

### Investigations

**Bedside (within 1 hour):**
- Check NEWS2, oxygen saturations, respiratory rate
- Blood cultures (two sets before antibiotics if not septic shock)
- Sputum for culture and sensitivity – but don’t delay antibiotics if septic

**Bloods:**
- FBC (neutrophilia), CRP, procalcitonin (elevated suggests bacterial)
- U&Es, LFTs (albumin low chronic infection), clotting (if drain planned)
- Blood cultures (aerobic + anaerobic)
- Consider: IGRA/T-SPOT (TB risk), HIV serology (in persistent/recurrent abscess)

**Imaging:**
- **First-line:** CXR PA and lateral – shows cavity with air-fluid level >2cm, often in posterior segments of upper lobes or superior segments of lower lobes (aspiration-prone areas). Look for associated consolidation or effusion.
- **Second-line:** CT chest with IV contrast (within 24-48h) – confirms cavity, differentiates from empyema, rules out endobronchial lesion (tumour, foreign body). Also assesses for bronchopleural fistula.
- **If TB suspected:** CT thorax with high-resolution windows + sputum AFB/NAAT.

**Special tests:**
- Bronchoscopy (if no improvement after 7-10 days of antibiotics, or if suspicion of malignancy/foreign body) – obtain BAL for culture, cytology, TB.

**Diagnostic criteria:** No single validated scoring system. Diagnosis relies on CXR/CT demonstration of a lung cavity with air-fluid level plus consistent clinical picture. Provideer criteria: >2cm cavity on CT, plus appropriate clinical setting (aspiration, fever, foul sputum).

**Never-miss test:** **CT chest with contrast** – the ONE investigation whose omission risks missing underlying malignancy or empyema, leading to failed treatment and delayed diagnosis.

### Management

**Immediate (first hour / ED):**
- If septic: IV fluids (crystalloid, 500ml bolus, reassess), start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (see Drugs section) after blood cultures – but do not delay >1 hour in sepsis.
- Ensure sputum sent for culture; if unable to produce, consider induced sputum.
- If massive haemoptysis: call senior, cross-match, consider bronchial artery embolisation – do not bronchoscope unstable patient.
- If tension pneumothorax: immediate needle decompression (2nd ICS, midclavicular line) followed by chest drain.

**Ward (first 24-72h):**
- Continue IV antibiotics for at least 72h or until afebrile and clinically improving.
- Chest physiotherapy with postural drainage (head-down, affected side up) – but only if no bronchopleural fistula or massive haemoptysis.
- Monitor sputum volume, colour, fever curve, and NEWS2 daily.
- If no improvement (fever, rising CRP) by day 7: re-image with CT, consider bronchoscopy, check for drug resistance or empyema.
- Consider CT-guided drainage if abscess >4cm and not responding to medical therapy (rare – most resolve with antibiotics alone).

**Long-term / outpatient:**
- Switch to oral antibiotics when afebrile >48h and clinically stable (see Drugs section).
- Total treatment duration: **4-6 weeks** (at least 2 weeks IV, then oral).
- Arrange follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks to confirm cavity resolution.
- Refer to respiratory physician if underlying malignancy or structural lung disease identified.

**Default Orders (if admitted):**
- **Fluids:** IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 1ml/kg/h (if not in fluid overload) – adjust based on clinical state.
- **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g. enoxaparin 40mg SC daily) unless bleeding risk (massive haemoptysis – withhold, discuss with senior).
- **Diet:** Full diet, encourage high-calorie/high-protein (weight loss common).
- **Observations:** 4-hourly NEWS2 initially; increase to hourly if NEWS2 ≥5.

### Drugs & Doses

**First-line agents (per BNF/UK practice):**
- **IV co-amoxiclav** (amoxicillin/clavulanate) 1.2g IV tds (if normal renal function) – covers anaerobes and gram-positive/negative.
- **Alternative if penicillin allergy:** IV clindamycin 600mg IV qds (anti-anaerobic) + IV cefuroxime 750mg tds (gram-negative cover) – but check local policy for dosing.
- **If severe sepsis or aspiration pneumonia with poor dentition:** IV piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5g tds – covers Pseudomonas and anaerobes.
- **Oral switch:** co-amoxiclav 625mg po tds or doxycycline 100mg po bd (if penicillin allergic) – continue for total of 4-6 weeks.

**Common alternatives:**
- Metronidazole 400mg po tds (add if anaerobes suspected and not on co-amoxiclav) – but clindamycin is more complete.
- Levofloxacin 500mg po od (reserve for severe allergies or resistance).

**Key contraindications / interactions:**
- Co-amoxiclav: caution in hepatic impairment (check LFTs), risk of cholestatic jaundice (especially elderly, courses >14 days).
- Clindamycin: risk of C. difficile colitis – monitor for diarrhoea.
- Piperacillin/tazobactam: high sodium load (4.5g contains 8mmol Na) – caution in heart failure.

**Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
- **Co-amoxiclav:** reduce dose if eGFR <30 (e.g. 1.2g IV stat then 600mg IV bd, or 375mg po bd). Check BNF.
- **Piperacillin/tazobactam:** adjust if eGFR <20 – [check local policy].
- **Clindamycin:** no renal adjustment needed; caution in severe liver disease (reduce dose).

**Most dangerous dose error:** **Not covering anaerobes** – giving only a cephalosporin or macrolide without anaerobic cover fails to treat the majority of lung abscesses, leading to clinical deterioration and empyema. Always add metronidazole or use co-amoxiclav.

### Complications

**Acute:**
- **Massive haemoptysis** (from erosion into bronchial artery) – can be fatal.
- **Bronchopleural fistula** → tension pneumothorax or empyema – need chest drain +/- surgery.
- **Empyema** (if abscess ruptures into pleural space) – requires chest drainage.
- **Septic emboli to brain** (especially if right-to-left shunt, e.g. from PFO) – look for neurological symptoms.

**Chronic:**
- Chronic cavity with persistent cough, weight loss, recurrent infections.
- Fibrothorax (if empyema complicated).
- Bronchiectasis (from chronic inflammation).

### Prognosis
- With appropriate intravenous antibiotics (4-6 weeks), >90% of uncomplicated lung abscesses resolve without drainage. Mortality ~5-10% overall, higher in elderly, immunocompromised, or if underlying malignancy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always examine the mouth** – poor dentition and gingivitis are the commonest sources of anaerobic bacteria in lung abscess. Document and consider dental consult.
- **If no improvement by day 7, escalate imaging** – CT is mandatory to exclude an endobronchial lesion (tumour/foreign body) or empyema that requires drainage.
- **Send sputum for culture before antibiotics** – but never delay antibiotics if septic; culture is often negative anyway (anaerobic difficult to culture). If negative, clinical response is key.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Using antibiotics without anaerobic cover** – lung abscess is almost always anaerobic; a cephalosporin or macrolide alone is insufficient. Always add metronidazole or use co-amoxiclav.
- **Stopping antibiotics early** – total course must be 4-6 weeks (IV then oral). Short courses (e.g. 7-10 days) risk relapse and chronic cavity.
- **Assuming all cavities are infection – not checking for underlying malignancy** – every patient with a lung abscess requires CT to exclude cancer, especially in smokers over 40. Do not attribute all symptoms to infection without imaging.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Worsening shortness of breath or haemoptysis (any amount)
  - Fever returns after being afebrile
  - New pleuritic chest pain or increasing cough with foul sputum
- **Follow-up:** Respiratory clinic in 4-6 weeks; repeat CXR at 6-8 weeks to confirm cavity closure. If cavity persists >12 weeks, consider bronchoscopy.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥5 with hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on FiO2 0.5) or rising lactate >2 mmol/L
  - Signs of massive haemoptysis (>200ml) or hypotension not responding to fluids
  - GCS drop <13 or new confusion
  - Suspected bronchopleural fistula (sudden dyspnoea, subcutaneous emphysema, new pneumothorax)
- **Specific thresholds:** Consider ICU referral if PaO2 <8 kPa on FiO2 0.5, or RR >30 despite best medical therapy. For haemoptysis, activate massive haemorrhage protocol (cross-match, call interventional radiology/surgery).
